old_claim.note: Oral Tradition & Storytelling provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct edge remains because the corrected node covers physical recognition and testing of exposed mineral sources; oral transmission of known source locations can be cultural context but is not a technical prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: Description and justification sections document red haematite/specularite extraction and stone mining tools at Ngwenya, but do not make oral storytelling part of the mineral-source recognition method.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source pins prospecting to physical mineral-source recognition and tool-supported extraction, not narrative transmission.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Keeping an oral-tradition prerequisite would confuse cultural memory of places with the technical ability to identify and test exposed mineral sources.

ontology_before: Prospecting inherited a generic oral-tradition enabling edge because knowledge transmission can support many ancient practices.
ontology_after: Prospecting is a physical mineral-source recognition node; oral tradition is not modeled as a direct prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: oral_tradition_storytelling is no longer a direct prerequisite for prospecting.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to traditional mineral-place lore rather than physical prospecting.
A direct source showed the earliest scoped prospecting practice depended on oral storytelling.
A downstream node required an oral-tradition path specifically through prospecting.
short_reason: Oral transmission can preserve source knowledge, but it is not a technical dependency for recognizing exposed minerals.
